title: Ezekiel
author: Ezekiel son of Buzi, a priest and prophet among the exiles in Babylon
setting: Among the first group of exiles by the Chebar canal in Babylon, before and after the fall of Jerusalem (586 BC). Ezekiel's visions are among the most elaborate in the Bible.
purpose: To explain the fall of Jerusalem as God's judgment on covenant unfaithfulness (God's glory departed — 8–11), to indict Israel's leaders and people, and to promise a breathtaking restoration — new heart, new spirit, new temple, and new creation.
themes: The glory of God (kavod YHWH) — departing and returning
The dry bones — resurrection and national restoration
The new covenant: a new heart and new spirit

outline.label: Ezekiel's Call and Warnings to Jerusalem
Restoration Oracles: Dry Bones, Shepherd, New Temple

key_people.name: Ezekiel ben Buzi
key_people.role: Priest-turned-prophet, deported to Babylon in 597 BC; his visions are the most visually elaborate in the OT and his ministry bridges the destruction of Jerusalem and the promise of its restoration

key_people.name: Nebuchadnezzar
key_people.role: The Babylonian king whose military campaigns against Jerusalem frame the first half of Ezekiel's ministry; referred to as God's instrument of judgment

key_people.name: The Prince/David
key_people.role: The future shepherd-king God promises in chapter 34 and 37 — a new David who will rule the restored people under God's direct kingship; a clear messianic figure

context: Ezekiel was a priest deported to Babylon with Jehoiachin in 597 BC, five years before Jerusalem's final fall. He prophesied among the exiles at Tel-abib from 593–571 BC. His ministry runs in three phases: judgment on Judah before the fall (chs. 1–24), oracles against surrounding nations (chs. 25–32), and restoration promises after the fall (chs. 33–48). The news of Jerusalem's fall (33:21) marks the pivotal turning point in his ministry.
christ_connection: Ezekiel's vision of the valley of dry bones (ch. 37) — dead Israel raised to life by God's Spirit — is the OT's most powerful image of resurrection; Paul draws on it when describing regeneration as life from the dead (Rom 6; Eph 2:1–7). The 'new heart and new spirit' promise (36:26–27) is fulfilled in the new birth by the Holy Spirit (John 3:5–8; Titus 3:5). The vision of the restored temple (chs. 40–48) with water flowing from its threshold (47:1–12) is taken up in Revelation 22 and applied to the New Jerusalem — Christ himself as the true temple.

key_verses.note: 'I will give you a new heart, and a new spirit I will put within you' — the promise of inner transformation that is the OT's clearest anticipation of regeneration by the Holy Spirit
'Son of man, can these bones live? And I answered, O Lord GOD, you know' — the vision of national resurrection that becomes the NT's image of spiritual resurrection from death in sin
Water flowing from the temple making everything live wherever it goes — the image of life-giving blessing from God's presence that Revelation 22 applies to the New Jerusalem

themes_detail.title: The Glory of God and Its Departure
themes_detail.text: The book's opening vision (chs. 1–3) is the most detailed and overwhelming theophany in the OT — a storm from the north, four living creatures, wheels within wheels, and the throne-chariot of God blazing with fire and rainbow light. This vision establishes that YHWH is not a local deity bound to Jerusalem but the universal sovereign. The departure of God's glory from the temple (10:18–19; 11:22–23) — step by step, reluctantly — is the most terrible event in the book: when God's presence leaves, the temple is already emptied of meaning before the Babylonians arrive.

themes_detail.title: Individual Responsibility Before God
themes_detail.text: Ezekiel 18 is a pivotal chapter for OT ethics: God explicitly denies the proverb 'the fathers have eaten sour grapes and the children's teeth are set on edge.' Each person stands before God in their own right; inherited guilt does not determine eternal destiny; and repentance at any point genuinely changes one's standing before God. This is not a denial of corporate solidarity but a clarification: the exile is not an arbitrary punishment passed from parents to children, but each generation (and each person) stands accountable for their own choices.

themes_detail.title: The New Covenant of Heart and Spirit
themes_detail.text: Ezekiel 36:24–28 is the most complete OT description of what conversion looks like from the divine side: God will gather, cleanse, give a new heart (replacing the stone heart of covenant resistance), put his Spirit within, and cause his people to walk in his statutes. This is not human self-improvement but divine transformation — God himself produces what he commands. Jesus alludes to this passage when speaking to Nicodemus about being 'born of water and the Spirit' (John 3:5), and Paul's description of regeneration in Ephesians 2 and Romans 6–8 is built on these chapters.

themes_detail.title: The Restored Temple and the Return of Glory
themes_detail.text: The final vision (chs. 40–48) is the most architecturally detailed in the Bible, describing a new temple with precise measurements, restored worship, redistributed land, and a renaming of the city: 'The LORD is there' (48:35). The vision is not a blueprint for a literal building but a theological portrait of what God's full presence with his people looks like. The water flowing from the temple threshold (47:1–12), deepening as it goes and making everything live, is the book's final image of what the restoration of God's glory means for the world — fulfilled ultimately in the New Jerusalem of Revelation 21–22.
